sourceMovie.title: The Lobster
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: The Lobster (2015) stuck because it commits to its comedy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Yorgos Lanthimos's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The comedy and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Swiss Army Man
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The comedy energy of The Lobster is alive in Swiss Army Man (2016), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a funny payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Seeking a Friend for the End of the World
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Seeking a Friend for the End of the World (2012) operates in the same comedy as The Lobster, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The funny payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Just Before I Go
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Just Before I Go (2015) belongs to the same conversation as The Lobster — both anchor their comedy in drama emotion that doesn't let go. The funny undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Life in a Year
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Lobster's drama stayed with you, Life in a Year (2020) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The romance through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Sightseers
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where The Lobster excels in comedy, Sightseers (2012) doubles down on romance to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Heaven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Heaven (2002) is the natural next step after The Lobster: same drama DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and romance elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Leave Her to Heaven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of The Lobster who respond to its drama will find Leave Her to Heaven (1945) immediately familiar. Both films share a romance sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: The Cabin in the Woods
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason The Lobster fans keep landing on The Cabin in the Woods (2012) — the comedy is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Greta
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Greta draws from the same drama well as The Lobster, channeling mystery in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2019 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Death and the Maiden
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Death and the Maiden (1994) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that The Lobster later expanded. If The Lobster resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Lobster?
faq.answer: Try Swiss Army Man, Seeking a Friend for the End of the World, Just Before I Go—each shares DNA with The Lobster while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Lobster?
faq.answer: The Lobster (2015) is best described as Comedy, Drama, Romance. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Lobster worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.0/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this comedy lane.
